content.text: I'll start by reading the architecture notes and the current implementation/test files.

content.content:
architecture-notes.md

example-mapping

node_modules

src

1	# Architecture Notes
2	
3	## After cycle 1
4	- Change: extracted module-level `const PROCESSING_FEE = 5` and used it in the return literal instead of a bare `5`.
5	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) — spec calls this "processing fee (R3)"; naming the literal makes the domain concept explicit and prepares for upcoming quote tests that all add +5 fee. Mass rose 2 → 3 but clarity trumps APP.
6	- Naming established: `PROCESSING_FEE` (module-private constant) for the fixed R3 fee component of a quote premium.
7	
8	## After cycle 2
9	- Change: renamed local `base` to `basePremium` inside `runScenario`'s step.map callback so it mirrors the module-level `BASE_PREMIUM` lookup table and reads explicitly in the premium formula.
10	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) — `base` alone is ambiguous (base of what?); `basePremium` matches the domain term used in the spec ("100 base") and ties directly to the `BASE_PREMIUM` map it is summed from. Mass unchanged at 30. Did not extract a `quoteStep` helper yet — too early with only 2 tests; will reconsider once item-modifier tests (curse, high-enchantment) land.
11	- Naming established: `basePremium` (local) for the sum of `BASE_PREMIUM[item.type]` across items in one quote step.
12	
13	## After cycle 3
14	- Change: extracted explaining variable `firstInsurance = basePremium * FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE` so the premium formula reads as a sum of three named domain components: `basePremium + firstInsurance + PROCESSING_FEE`.
15	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) — the spec describes "first insurance" as a distinct premium component (10% of base), parallel to `PROCESSING_FEE`; the formula now mirrors that vocabulary directly. Mass +1 (one binding) but Rule 2 > APP per project rules. This sets up cleanly for upcoming additive components (curse surcharge, high-enchantment surcharge, loyalty discount) — each will become its own named line in the same shape.
16	- Naming established: `firstInsurance` (local) for the 10% first-insurance premium component on `basePremium`.
17	
18	## After cycle 4
19	- No refactoring (justified): Green added one row (`staff: 80`) to the existing `BASE_PREMIUM` table — purely additive data, no structural change. All names still reveal intent (`BASE_PREMIUM`, `basePremium`, `firstInsurance`, `PROCESSING_FEE` match spec vocabulary), no duplication present, formula reads as three named domain components. Extracting a `quoteStep`/`computeBasePremium` helper is still premature: per cycle-2 plan, defer until item-modifier tests (curse, high-enchantment) land — the next todo (`potion`) is yet another base-premium row, not a modifier. Touching the `?? 0` fallback now would pre-empt the dedicated CLI-error tests for unknown items further down the list.
20	
21	## After cycle 5
22	- No refactoring (justified): Green added one row (`potion: 40`) to `BASE_PREMIUM` — same purely-additive shape as cycle 4. No new structural opportunities emerged. Names still match spec vocabulary; no duplication; the three-component formula (basePremium + firstInsurance + PROCESSING_FEE) still reads as the spec describes. Inlining the `results` binding would save 1 mass unit but hurt Rule 2 (the named `results` mirrors the output key). Extracting `computeBasePremium(items)` is still premature — the natural trigger is the 3-rune block-discount test (cycle 8 in the todo list), where pure lookup-and-sum first breaks down; refactoring now would be guessing at the helper's shape before seeing how component blocks fit. The `?? 0` fallback continues to be reserved for the CLI unknown-item-type error test later.
23	
24	## After cycle 6
25	- Change: extracted explaining variable `rawPremium = basePremium + firstInsurance + PROCESSING_FEE` so the line `const premium = Math.ceil(rawPremium)` reads as a discrete rounding step.
26	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) — the rune test (32.5 → 33) made rounding-up a distinct domain step (spec R3: "rounded up to the nearest G"). Naming the unrounded total separates "compose the premium from its named components" from "round up to whole G"; both intents now stand out at a glance. Mass +1 (one binding) but Rule 2 > APP. Did not extract a `quoteStep`/`computeBasePremium` helper yet — per cycles 2/4/5, that's reserved for the 3-rune block-discount test where pure lookup-and-sum breaks down; the next test (2 runes, no block) is still pure summation.
27	- Naming established: `rawPremium` (local) for the unrounded sum of premium components, prior to round-up.
28	
29	## After cycle 7
30	- No refactoring (justified): Green made no code change — the 2-runes test auto-passed via the existing reduce+lookup (25+25=50 base, ×1.1=55, +5=60). Six candidates were considered and rejected: (1) extract `computeQuotePremium(items)` helper — natural trigger is the 3-rune block-discount test (cycle 8 per the todo list) where pure lookup-and-sum breaks down; extracting now is still guessing at the helper's shape, same reasoning as cycles 2/4/5/6; (2) inline `rawPremium` — would reverse cycle 6's Rule 2 win; (3) inline `results` — already rejected in cycle 5 on Rule 2 grounds (mirrors output-key vocabulary); (4) inline the `scenario` cast — named cast on its own line is the conventional TS narrowing pattern; (5) tighten `runScenario(input: unknown)` signature — reserved for the CLI-error tests further down the list; (6) extract the reducer lambda — single call site, two short tokens, and the `?? 0` is tied to the deferred unknown-item-type CLI-error test. All names still match spec vocabulary; mass remains around 41 with no Rule-2-respecting reduction available.
31	
32	## After cycle 8
33	- Change: named the two magic numbers in the rune block-discount branch — introduced module-level `const RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE = 3` and `const RUNE_BLOCK_PRICE = 60`, and used them in the `if (type === "rune" && count === RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE) basePremium += RUNE_BLOCK_PRICE` branch.
34	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) — Green introduced bare `3` and `60` for spec R2 (rune block discount); naming them ties the branch directly to spec vocabulary ("block of 3 runes" → "block price"). Mass +4 (two bindings + two constants at module scope) but Rule 2 > APP per project rules. Deferred again: extracting a `computeBasePremium(items)` / `priceForType(type, count)` helper or a table-driven block-discount rule — the next two tests ("4 runes no block", "3 runes + 3 moonstones") will reshape the conditional from `count === 3` into "count of complete 3-blocks across alike-typed components"; refactoring now would guess at that shape. Also deferred: renaming `perItem` (short name, 6-line scope — clear enough), tightening `runScenario(input: unknown)` (reserved for CLI-error tests).
35	- Naming established: `RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE` (module-private constant) for the spec-R2 block size of 3 runes; `RUNE_BLOCK_PRICE` (module-private constant) for the price of one complete rune block.
36	
37	## After cycle 9
38	- Change: moved `const perItem = BASE_PREMIUM[type] ?? 0` from the top of the for-loop into the `else` branch where it is actually used; the block branch (`count === RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE`) no longer computes a value it ignores.
39	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) — `perItem` now sits next to its only consumer (`basePremium += perItem * count`), making the else-branch read as a self-contained per-item pricing step; the if-branch reads as a self-contained block-priced step. Rule 4 (Fewest Elements) — the block branch no longer carries a dead binding/lookup. APP static element count is unchanged (~64) since the elements still exist in the else-branch, but the dead path in the block branch is gone. Did NOT extract `computeBasePremium(items)` / `priceForType(type, count)` helper or generalize the block rule to `Math.floor(count / RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE)` arithmetic — both are still premature per cycles 2/4/5/6/7/8: the natural trigger is "3 runes + 3 moonstones" (cycle 11 in the todo list), where the conditional MUST generalize from "type === rune" to "any block-eligible type" and from `count === 3` to "complete 3-blocks of one type". Refactoring now would guess at that shape; the 7-rune test (cycle 10) still falls through the else-branch cleanly (7*25=175). Locality move is reversible if the block rule restructures the branch entirely.
40	- Naming established: none new (`perItem` retained in narrower scope).
41	
42	## After cycle 10
43	- No refactoring (justified): Green made no code change — the 7-runes test auto-passed via the existing else-branch summation (7 × 25 = 175 base, ×1.1 = 192.5, +5 = 197.5 → ceil 198). The test exercised the same `BASE_PREMIUM[type] * count` path that the 4-runes test already covered (cycle 9), so no new domain concept or structural pressure emerged. Six candidates considered and rejected: (1) extract `computeBasePremium(items)` / `priceForType(type, count)` helper — natural trigger is "3 runes + 3 moonstones" (next test in the todo list), where the conditional MUST generalize from `type === "rune"` to "any block-eligible type"; extracting now would guess at the helper's shape, same reasoning as cycles 2/4/5/6/7/8/9; (2) generalize block rule to `Math.floor(count / RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE) * RUNE_BLOCK_PRICE + (count % RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE) * perItem` arithmetic — the 7-rune test actually CONFIRMS the rule is "exactly one full block of 3 → block price; else per-item" (7 runes priced at 7×25, not 60+4×25=160), so block-arithmetic would over-fit and break the 4-rune and 7-rune tests; (3) rename `perItem` to `perItemPrice` — lives in a 2-line scope, multiplied by `count` immediately, rename adds mass with no Rule-2 win; (4) inline `rawPremium` / `firstInsurance` / `basePremium` — reverses cycles 3/6 Rule-2 wins; (5) tighten `runScenario(input: unknown)` signature — reserved for CLI-error tests further down the list; (6) extract the count-by-type loop into `countByType(items)` — single call site, 3 lines, well-named local; adds invocation mass without Rule-2 win, defer until a second call site appears (claim-handling tests may bring one). All names still match spec vocabulary; mass remains around 65 with no Rule-2-respecting reduction available.
44	
45	## After cycle 11
46	- No refactoring (justified): Green added one row (`moonstone: 25`) to `BASE_PREMIUM` — purely additive data, same shape as cycles 4/5. The mixed-type test ("2 runes + 1 moonstone → base 75 G, no block") was the first to exercise the per-type iteration with two distinct types, and it CONFIRMED the existing block branch is correctly gated on `type === "rune"` (the moonstone fell through the else-branch and produced 25, never colliding with the block path). Eight candidates considered and rejected: (1) extract `computeBasePremium(items)` / `priceForType(type, count)` helper — natural trigger is now the very next test, "3 runes + 3 moonstones → two separate blocks", which will force the block branch to generalize from `type === "rune"` to "any block-eligible type" (or all types); extracting now would guess at that shape, same reasoning as cycles 2/4/5/6/7/8/9/10; (2) replace `if (type === "rune" && count === RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE)` with a `BLOCK_PRICES: Record<string, number>` table — premature; cycle 11 doesn't yet constrain whether moonstones or other types have block discounts, the next test will; (3) generalize block to "any type at count === RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE" — would over-fit before "3 runes + 3 moonstones" determines whether moonstones share the rule; (4) inline `countsByType` into a reduce — single use, the for-loop reads explicitly, reduce form adds invocation + arrow mass with no Rule-2 win; (5) extract the per-step `.map` callback into named `quoteStep(step)` — single call site, and the next test will likely reshape the type-counting/block logic inside it; freezing a shape now is premature; (6) rename `perItem` to `perItemPrice` — 2-line scope, multiplied by `count` on the very next line, rename adds 1 binding with no clarity win (re-rejected from cycle 10); (7) tighten `runScenario(input: unknown)` signature — reserved for CLI-error tests; (8) inline `rawPremium` / `firstInsurance` / `basePremium` — reverses cycles 3/6 Rule-2 wins. All names still match spec vocabulary; mass remains around 85 (one extra row in BASE_PREMIUM, +2 elements vs cycle 10) with no Rule-2-respecting reduction available.
47	
48	## After cycle 12
49	- Change: renamed `RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE` → `COMPONENT_BLOCK_SIZE` and `RUNE_BLOCK_PRICE` → `COMPONENT_BLOCK_PRICE`; grouped the three component-related constants together with `COMPONENT_TYPES` first, then size, then price.
50	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) — Green generalized the block rule from "rune-only" to "any type in `COMPONENT_TYPES`" (both runes and moonstones now form blocks). The `RUNE_*` prefix on the constants no longer matches the rule they govern: the if-branch reads `COMPONENT_TYPES.has(type) && count === RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE` — a conceptual mismatch where rune-prefixed constants are applied to moonstones. Renaming to `COMPONENT_*` aligns the constants with the spec term ("components") and with the already-correctly-named `COMPONENT_TYPES` set. Mass unchanged at ~83 (pure identifier rename). Other candidates re-considered and rejected: (a) replace the `if (COMPONENT_TYPES.has(type) && count === COMPONENT_BLOCK_SIZE)` with a table-driven `BLOCK_RULES: Record<string, {size, price}>` — premature; current spec has identical block rules for both component types, so a record adds invocation + lookup mass with no Rule-2 win until a third component type (or differing block rules) appears; (b) generalize block arithmetic to `Math.floor(count / size) * blockPrice + (count % size) * perItem` — same over-fit risk flagged in cycle 10; the spec explicitly defines the block as "exactly count === size", confirmed by the 4-rune and 7-rune tests; (c) extract `computeBasePremium(items)` helper — still single call site, and the next test class (item modifiers: curse, high-enchantment) will introduce per-item surcharges that reshape the inner loop, so freezing a helper shape now is premature; (d) tighten `runScenario(input: unknown)` signature — reserved for CLI-error tests further down the list.
51	- Naming established: `COMPONENT_BLOCK_SIZE` (module-private constant) for the spec-R2 block size shared by all component types; `COMPONENT_BLOCK_PRICE` (module-private constant) for the price of one complete component block. The `RUNE_*` names are retired.
52	
53	## After cycle 13
54	- Change: named the bare `0.5` literal in the curse-surcharge line as a module-level `const CURSED_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.5`, placed alongside `FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE`. The line now reads `cursedSurcharge += (BASE_PREMIUM[item.type] ?? 0) * CURSED_SURCHARGE_RATE`.
55	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) — Green introduced a bare `0.5` for spec R4 (cursed surcharge = 50% of base premium); naming it ties the literal to spec vocabulary and makes the curse line read in parallel with the existing `basePremium * FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE` line (same `value * RATE` shape for both spec-defined percentages). Mass +2 (one binding + one constant at module scope, minus one inline constant) but Rule 2 > APP per project rules. This sets up the upcoming high-enchantment surcharge test (cycle 14) to follow the same pattern (likely `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE = 30` or similar). Other candidates re-considered and rejected: (a) extract `priceForType(type)` / `baseOf(item)` helper for the repeated `BASE_PREMIUM[type] ?? 0` lookup — appears twice (else-branch `perItem`, curse loop); natural trigger is the high-enchantment test which will add a third per-item modifier, at which point a per-item modifier helper (`itemSurcharges(item, basePremium)`) becomes the right shape — extracting now would guess; (b) merge the two passes over `step.items` (block-count aggregation + curse) into one — couples two different domain concerns (per-type vs per-item) and Green explicitly chose the two-pass shape for clarity; (c) extract `quoteStep(step)` callback — single call site, still being reshaped by upcoming modifier tests; (d) tighten `runScenario(input: unknown)` — reserved for CLI-error tests; (e) inline `cursedSurcharge` into the rawPremium sum — would reverse the cycle-3/6 pattern of naming each premium component on its own line.
56	- Naming established: `CURSED_SURCHARGE_RATE` (module-private constant) for the spec-R4 curse surcharge percentage (50% of cursed item's base premium).
57	
58	## After cycle 14
59	- Change: unified the two parallel per-item surcharge passes (cursed and high-enchantment) into a single loop over `step.items`. One accumulator `itemSurcharges` replaces `cursedSurcharge` + `highEnchantmentSurcharge`; one shared `itemBase = BASE_PREMIUM[item.type] ?? 0` lookup serves both modifier checks. The premium sum becomes `basePremium + itemSurcharges + firstInsurance + PROCESSING_FEE`.
60	- Rationale: Rule 3 (No Duplication) — the two passes shared identical structure (iterate `step.items`, look up `BASE_PREMIUM[item.type] ?? 0`, accumulate `base * RATE`); cycle 13 explicitly flagged that the natural trigger for unification was "a third per-item modifier or a compound test." Cycle 14's Green added the high-enchantment surcharge, making the duplication concrete (two passes both doing the same lookup on the same item). Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) also wins: the single loop reads as "for each item, accumulate its modifier surcharges" — each modifier on its own line within the loop, parallel structure made explicit by code rather than visual similarity. Rule 4 (Fewest Elements) — one loop instead of two, one accumulator instead of two, one BASE_PREMIUM lookup per item instead of two. Mass dropped substantially (~35 → ~24 in the modifier block) by eliminating one for-loop (-5), one binding (-1), one lookup invocation pair (-2 via dedup), and one literal `0` (-1). This also pre-positions the upcoming "cursed steel sword enchantment 5" test (both modifiers on one item): with the two-pass shape it would iterate the same item twice; with the unified pass, both surcharges accumulate in a single visit. Did NOT extract `itemSurcharge(item, base)` helper — the two `if` lines are short, tied to module-level rates, and live in a single 8-line scope; extracting would add an invocation per call with no Rule-2 win until a third modifier appears.
61	- Naming established: `itemSurcharges` (local) for the sum of per-item modifier surcharges on a quote step; `itemBase` (local) for the cached `BASE_PREMIUM[item.type] ?? 0` used by all modifier rates on one item. The names `cursedSurcharge` and `highEnchantmentSurcharge` are retired (their semantic role is now line-local inside the unified loop).
62	
63	## After cycle 15
64	- Change: extracted module-private `function computeBasePremium(items: Item[]): number` containing the per-type counting loop, the block-discount branch, and the per-item fall-through summation; replaced the 10 lines inside the `.map` callback with a single named call `const basePremium = computeBasePremium(step.items)`.
65	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) — the `.map` callback now reads as three named domain steps: "compute base premium for items" → "accumulate per-item surcharges" → "first insurance + fee + round up." The base-premium step's name now documents the spec-R1/R2 role (per-type aggregation with block discount) that was previously implicit in 10 lines of indented code. Rule 3 (No Duplication) — establishes a clean seam: the deferred claim-tests insurance-sum function ("policy with sword + 3 runes → insurance sum 1750 (block does not change insurance sum)") will live alongside `computeBasePremium` as a sibling helper that shares the per-type counting structure but skips the block discount; extracting now lets the two helpers stand parallel rather than forcing a later split of intertwined code. Rule 4 (Fewest Elements) — the `.map` callback drops from ~25 to ~15 lines, sharpening its identity as "one quote step's premium pipeline." Mass: the call adds 1 invocation (+2) and a function definition adds parameter binding (+1), but the inlined body's locals (`countsByType`, the for-of and for-of/object-entries loops) move from `.map`-scope to function-scope with no net component change — so total static mass is approximately unchanged (~95–97). The trigger this cycle: the cycle-15 threshold test (enchantment 4) auto-passed and added no structural pressure, but the base-premium logic itself has now been stable for THREE consecutive cycles (cycles 12 finalized constants, 13–14 reshaped only the surcharge path, 15 added no change). Per cycles 2/4/5/6/7/8/10/11's deferral rationale, the natural trigger for extracting `computeBasePremium` was "the block logic settles AND a forward sibling (insurance sum) needs the same per-type counting shape." Both conditions are now met: block logic settled (no remaining component-tests in the immediate todo list reshape it); insurance-sum tests are visible in the todo list and explicitly diverge from base-with-block on the block-discount line. Did NOT also extract `quoteStep(step)` callback yet — its signature is about to change when loyalty (yearsWithMHPCO) lands; freezing it now would force an immediate signature change. Did NOT extract `BASE_PREMIUM[item.type] ?? 0` helper — the `?? 0` fallback is still reserved for the CLI unknown-item-type error test further down the list.
66	- Naming established: `computeBasePremium(items)` (module-private function) for the spec-R1/R2 base premium of one quote step's items (per-type aggregation including block discount for component types). This is the block-aware variant; the future `insuranceSum(items)` helper (claim-tests) will be its block-unaware sibling.
67

content.text: Now I have a clear picture. Cycle 16's Green added no code change — the "cursed steel sword enchantment 5" test auto-passed since the unified per-item surcharge loop (from cycle 14) already handles both modifiers on one item correctly. Let me run tests to confirm green state.

input.description: Run tests to verify green state

content.text:
16 tests passing. Now let me evaluate refactoring opportunities for this cycle.

## Refactor Phase Analysis

### Step 1: Naming Evaluation (First Priority)

Current names:
- `runScenario` — clear top-level entry
- `computeBasePremium` — accurate (returns the spec-R1/R2 base premium)
- `itemSurcharges`, `itemBase`, `firstInsurance`, `rawPremium`, `basePremium` — all match spec vocabulary
- `BASE_PREMIUM`, `COMPONENT_TYPES`, `COMPONENT_BLOCK_SIZE`, `COMPONENT_BLOCK_PRICE`, `PROCESSING_FEE`, `FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE`, `CURSED_SURCHARGE_RATE`, `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_RATE`, `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD` — all align with spec vocabulary

The cycle-16 test ("cursed steel sword enchantment 5") was the first to exercise BOTH per-item modifiers on the SAME item simultaneously. The unified per-item loop now reads as "for each item, accumulate all its applicable modifier surcharges" — a clear naming win from cycle 14. No rename needed.

### Step 2: Initial APP Mass

Module-level: 9 constants + 1 record (6 entries) + 1 set (2 entries) ≈ 25 elements.

Per-step `.map` body: ~6 bindings, 1 invocation (computeBasePremium), 1 loop, 2 conditionals, ~6 invocations (Math.ceil, +=, lookup), ~8 binding refs. Around 50 mass.

`computeBasePremium`: 2 loops, ~6 bindings, 1 conditional, ~10 invocations. Around 45 mass.

Total mass: ~95 (unchanged from cycle 15).

### Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules

**Rule 1 (Tests Pass):** ✅ All 16 tests passing.

**Rule 2 (Reveals Intent):** Evaluating the per-item surcharge loop:

```typescript
let itemSurcharges = 0;
for (const item of step.items) {
  const itemBase = BASE_PREMIUM[item.type] ?? 0;
  if (item.cursed) {
    itemSurcharges += itemBase * CURSED_SURCHARGE_RATE;
  }
  if ((item.enchantment ?? 0) >= HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD) {
    itemSurcharges += itemBase * HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_RATE;
  }
}
```

This is the FIRST cycle where both modifiers fire on the same item. The unified loop reads well, but evaluating candidates:

(a) **Extract `computeItemSurcharges(items: Item[]): number`** — sibling helper to `computeBasePremium`. The `.map` callback would then read as a clean 3-line pipeline: `basePremium = computeBasePremium(...)` → `surcharges = computeItemSurcharges(...)` → `premium = ceil(basePremium + surcharges + firstInsurance + fee)`. This makes the `.map` callback read as four named domain steps and brings parity with `computeBasePremium` (both are "compute X from items"). Cycle 14 deferred this on the grounds that "two `if` lines are short, tied to module-level rates, and live in a single 8-line scope; extracting would add an invocation per call with no Rule-2 win until a third modifier appears" — but cycle 15 then extracted `computeBasePremium` precisely on the parallel-sibling principle (sets up insurance-sum). Now that `computeBasePremium` exists, the `.map` callback has an asymmetry: base premium is a named helper call but surcharges are an inlined 9-line loop. Symmetry with `computeBasePremium` is a Rule 2 win.

(b) **Extract `pricePremium(step)` for the whole `.map` callback** — premature; signature is about to change with loyalty (yearsWithMHPCO).

(c) **Extract `BASE_PREMIUM[type] ?? 0` into `baseOf(type)` helper** — still reserved for the CLI unknown-item-type error test.

(d) **Tighten `runScenario(input: unknown)` signature** — reserved for CLI-error tests.

(e) **Inline `itemBase`** — would reverse cycle 14's locality-and-clarity win; with two modifier checks it's still useful as a cached lookup name.

Cycle 16 is the natural trigger for (a): the "cursed enchantment 5" test confirmed the unified per-item surcharge loop handles compound modifiers correctly, which is the closure-of-modifier-behavior signal that the surcharge step has stabilized. Extracting now creates parallel structure with `computeBasePremium`, and the upcoming "cursed sword + plain amulet" test (multi-item modifier scope) will exercise it on a different shape (different items, different modifiers) without changing the helper's signature — making this the right moment.

**Rule 3 (No Duplication):** No duplication present.

**Rule 4 (Fewest Elements):** Extracting `computeItemSurcharges` adds a function declaration (+ small mass) but removes 1 binding (`itemSurcharges` is no longer a local accumulator) and consolidates the 9-line block under a named identity. Net mass approximately neutral; Rule 2 win is the driver.

### Step 4: Implement Refactoring

content.text:
All 16 tests still pass. Now let me update the architecture notes.

### Step 5: Recalculate APP Mass

The `.map` callback now has 5 named bindings, 2 helper invocations, 2 arithmetic operations, 1 invocation (Math.ceil), 1 object literal — clean linear pipeline. The 9-line surcharge loop moved into a dedicated function — same elements, but now identified by a name. Net mass roughly neutral (~95).

### Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied**:
- ✅ Extracted `computeItemSurcharges(items: Item[]): number` as sibling of `computeBasePremium`.
- ✅ The `.map` callback now reads as four named domain steps: `basePremium` → `itemSurcharges` → `firstInsurance` → `premium` (ceil of sum + fee).
- ✅ Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): Restores symmetry — both "compute X from items" steps are now named helpers rather than one named and one inlined.
- ✅ Mass change: approximately neutral (~95 → ~95); the surcharge-loop elements moved into a named function rather than disappearing.

## After cycle 15
- Change: extracted module-private `function computeBasePremium(items: Item[]): number` containing the per-type counting loop, the block-discount branch, and the per-item fall-through summation; replaced the 10 lines inside the `.map` callback with a single named call `const basePremium = computeBasePremium(step.items)`.
- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) — the `.map` callback now reads as three named domain steps: "compute base premium for items" → "accumulate per-item surcharges" → "first insurance + fee + round up." The base-premium step's name now documents the spec-R1/R2 role (per-type aggregation with block discount) that was previously implicit in 10 lines of indented code. Rule 3 (No Duplication) — establishes a clean seam: the deferred claim-tests insurance-sum function ("policy with sword + 3 runes → insurance sum 1750 (block does not change insurance sum)") will live alongside `computeBasePremium` as a sibling helper that shares the per-type counting structure but skips the block discount; extracting now lets the two helpers stand parallel rather than forcing a later split of intertwined code. Rule 4 (Fewest Elements) — the `.map` callback drops from ~25 to ~15 lines, sharpening its identity as "one quote step's premium pipeline." Mass: the call adds 1 invocation (+2) and a function definition adds parameter binding (+1), but the inlined body's locals (`countsByType`, the for-of and for-of/object-entries loops) move from `.map`-scope to function-scope with no net component change — so total static mass is approximately unchanged (~95–97). The trigger this cycle: the cycle-15 threshold test (enchantment 4) auto-passed and added no structural pressure, but the base-premium logic itself has now been stable for THREE consecutive cycles (cycles 12 finalized constants, 13–14 reshaped only the surcharge path, 15 added no change). Per cycles 2/4/5/6/7/8/10/11's deferral rationale, the natural trigger for extracting `computeBasePremium` was "the block logic settles AND a forward sibling (insurance sum) needs the same per-type counting shape." Both conditions are now met: block logic settled (no remaining component-tests in the immediate todo list reshape it); insurance-sum tests are visible in the todo list and explicitly diverge from base-with-block on the block-discount line. Did NOT also extract `quoteStep(step)` callback yet — its signature is about to change when loyalty (yearsWithMHPCO) lands; freezing it now would force an immediate signature change. Did NOT extract `BASE_PREMIUM[item.type] ?? 0` helper — the `?? 0` fallback is still reserved for the CLI unknown-item-type error test further down the list.
- Naming established: `computeBasePremium(items)` (module-private function) for the spec-R1/R2 base premium of one quote step's items (per-type aggregation including block discount for component types). This is the block-aware variant; the future `insuranceSum(items)` helper (claim-tests) will be its block-unaware sibling.

## After cycle 15
- Change: extracted module-private `function computeBasePremium(items: Item[]): number` containing the per-type counting loop, the block-discount branch, and the per-item fall-through summation; replaced the 10 lines inside the `.map` callback with a single named call `const basePremium = computeBasePremium(step.items)`.
- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) — the `.map` callback now reads as three named domain steps: "compute base premium for items" → "accumulate per-item surcharges" → "first insurance + fee + round up." The base-premium step's name now documents the spec-R1/R2 role (per-type aggregation with block discount) that was previously implicit in 10 lines of indented code. Rule 3 (No Duplication) — establishes a clean seam: the deferred claim-tests insurance-sum function ("policy with sword + 3 runes → insurance sum 1750 (block does not change insurance sum)") will live alongside `computeBasePremium` as a sibling helper that shares the per-type counting structure but skips the block discount; extracting now lets the two helpers stand parallel rather than forcing a later split of intertwined code. Rule 4 (Fewest Elements) — the `.map` callback drops from ~25 to ~15 lines, sharpening its identity as "one quote step's premium pipeline." Mass: the call adds 1 invocation (+2) and a function definition adds parameter binding (+1), but the inlined body's locals (`countsByType`, the for-of and for-of/object-entries loops) move from `.map`-scope to function-scope with no net component change — so total static mass is approximately unchanged (~95–97). The trigger this cycle: the cycle-15 threshold test (enchantment 4) auto-passed and added no structural pressure, but the base-premium logic itself has now been stable for THREE consecutive cycles (cycles 12 finalized constants, 13–14 reshaped only the surcharge path, 15 added no change). Per cycles 2/4/5/6/7/8/10/11's deferral rationale, the natural trigger for extracting `computeBasePremium` was "the block logic settles AND a forward sibling (insurance sum) needs the same per-type counting shape." Both conditions are now met: block logic settled (no remaining component-tests in the immediate todo list reshape it); insurance-sum tests are visible in the todo list and explicitly diverge from base-with-block on the block-discount line. Did NOT also extract `quoteStep(step)` callback yet — its signature is about to change when loyalty (yearsWithMHPCO) lands; freezing it now would force an immediate signature change. Did NOT extract `BASE_PREMIUM[item.type] ?? 0` helper — the `?? 0` fallback is still reserved for the CLI unknown-item-type error test further down the list.
- Naming established: `computeBasePremium(items)` (module-private function) for the spec-R1/R2 base premium of one quote step's items (per-type aggregation including block discount for component types). This is the block-aware variant; the future `insuranceSum(items)` helper (claim-tests) will be its block-unaware sibling.

## After cycle 16
- Change: extracted module-private `function computeItemSurcharges(items: Item[]): number` containing the per-item modifier loop (curse + high-enchantment surcharges); replaced the 9-line loop inside the `.map` callback with a single named call `const itemSurcharges = computeItemSurcharges(step.items)`.
- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) — cycle 14's unification of the two per-item modifier passes left a 9-line accumulator loop inside the `.map` callback, asymmetric with the already-extracted `computeBasePremium` (which is also "compute X from items"). The cycle-16 test ("cursed steel sword enchantment 5", both modifiers on one item) was the closure-of-modifier-behavior signal: every spec-R4/R5 modifier combination at the per-item level is now exercised (none, curse only, ench≥5 only, both). The `.map` callback now reads as four named domain steps — `basePremium` → `itemSurcharges` → `firstInsurance` → `premium` (ceil of sum + fee) — with the two "compute X from items" steps as parallel-named sibling helpers (rather than one named and one inlined). This matches cycle 15's reasoning for `computeBasePremium`: stable shape + sibling parallelism = right moment to extract. Cycle 14 explicitly deferred this extraction with "defer until a third modifier appears OR a compound test"; the cycle-16 compound test plus stability across cycles 13/14/15/16 on the modifier path satisfies the deferred trigger. Rule 4 (Fewest Elements) — the `.map` callback drops from ~15 to ~8 lines, sharpening its identity as "compose one quote step's premium from named components." Mass: the call adds 1 invocation (+2) and a function definition adds parameter binding (+1), but the inlined body's locals (`surcharges` accumulator, the for-of loop, `itemBase` cache, two `if` branches) move from `.map`-scope to function-scope with no net component change — total static mass is approximately unchanged (~95). Did NOT extract `quoteStep(step)` callback wrapping the four steps — its signature is about to change when loyalty (yearsWithMHPCO) lands (the next test in the todo list); freezing it now would force an immediate signature change. Did NOT extract `BASE_PREMIUM[item.type] ?? 0` helper — `?? 0` fallback still reserved for the CLI unknown-item-type error test. Renamed the accumulator from `itemSurcharges` (cycle 14's name) to `surcharges` inside the new helper since the function's name already says "item surcharges"; the call site retains `itemSurcharges` as the local binding to keep the named-component formula readable.
- Naming established: `computeItemSurcharges(items)` (module-private function) for the spec-R4/R5 per-item modifier surcharges (curse + high-enchantment) summed across all items in one quote step. Sibling of `computeBasePremium` — both have signature `(items: Item[]) => number`, both compute one named premium component. The local `surcharges` inside the helper retires the `itemSurcharges`-as-accumulator role from cycle 14.
